eli3: PCA is a mathematical technique that finds the directions of maximum variance in data. Imagine rotating your data until the spread is largest along the horizontal axis — that's your first principal component.
Mathematically, PCA diagonalizes the covariance matrix. The principal components are the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix, and the eigenvalues tell you how much variance each component captures.
The data is centered (mean subtracted), then projected onto the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix. This gives a new coordinate system where axes are uncorrelated and ordered by importance.

whenWhy.title: Decorrelating features
whenWhy.body: PCA transforms correlated features into uncorrelated principal components. The covariance matrix becomes diagonal. This simplifies modeling and removes multicollinearity issues in linear regression.

whenWhy.title: Optimal linear compression
whenWhy.body: PCA gives the best linear low-dimensional approximation in terms of reconstruction error (Eckart-Young-Mirsky). The first k principal components minimize ||X - X̂||_F² among all rank-k approximations.

whenWhy.title: Whitening data
whenWhy.body: PCA + scaling by 1/√λ (whitening) transforms data to have identity covariance. This is a preprocessing step for ICA, some neural network architectures, and when features have very different scales.

whenWhy.title: Feature extraction
whenWhy.body: Instead of original features, use principal component scores as features. This can improve downstream models by removing noise (small variance components) and reducing dimensionality.

deepDiveIntro: Given centered data X ∈ ℝⁿˣᵖ (n samples, p features), the sample covariance is S = (1/(n-1)) XᵀX. PCA finds the eigendecomposition S = VΛVᵀ where Λ = diag(λ₁,…,λₚ) with λ₁ ≥ … ≥ λₚ and V has orthonormal eigenvectors as columns.

The principal components are the columns of V. The k-th principal component score for sample i is zᵢₖ = xᵢᵀvₖ. The variance of the k-th score equals λₖ. Geometrically, PCA finds the best-fitting k-dimensional affine subspace in terms of least squares reconstruction error.

deepDive.heading: Covariance Matrix
deepDive.body: For centered X, the sample covariance S = (1/(n-1)) XᵀX is symmetric positive semi-definite. Its entries Sᵢⱼ measure how features i and j vary together. Diagonal entries are variances; off-diagonal are covariances.

deepDive.heading: Eigendecomposition
deepDive.body: S = VΛVᵀ. Columns of V are eigenvectors (principal component directions). Diagonal of Λ contains eigenvalues (variances along each PC). SVD of X gives the same: X = UΣVᵀ, then S = (1/(n-1)) VΣ²Vᵀ, so λᵢ = σᵢ²/(n-1).
deepDive.formula: Svᵢ = λᵢvᵢ, λᵢ = variance along vᵢ

deepDive.heading: Projection and Reconstruction
deepDive.body: Project data onto first k PCs: Z = XVₖ where Vₖ has first k eigenvectors. Reconstruction: X̂ = ZVₖᵀ = XVₖVₖᵀ. The projection matrix Pₖ = VₖVₖᵀ is orthogonal (Pₖ² = Pₖ, Pₖᵀ = Pₖ).
deepDive.formula: Z = XVₖ, X̂ = ZVₖᵀ, reconstruction error = Σᵢ₌ₖ₊₁ᵖ λᵢ

deepDive.heading: Variance Explained
deepDive.body: Total variance = tr(S) = Σ λᵢ. The k-th PC explains λₖ/Σλᵢ of total variance. Cumulative explained variance = Σᵢ₌₁ᵏ λᵢ / Σᵢ₌₁ᵖ λᵢ. Often plot this to choose k (elbow method or threshold like 95%).

deepDive.heading: PCA as Optimization
deepDive.body: First PC: v₁ = argmax_{||v||=1} vᵀSv = argmax Var(Xv). This is a Rayleigh quotient maximization. Subsequent PCs solve the same problem orthogonal to previous components. This is a sequence of constrained quadratic optimizations.
deepDive.formula: v₁ = argmax_{||v||=1} vᵀSv, vₖ = argmax_{||v||=1, v⟂v₁,...,vₖ₋₁} vᵀSv

takeaways: PCA finds eigenvectors of covariance matrix S = (1/(n-1)) XᵀX.
Eigenvalues λᵢ are variances along principal component directions.
Projection onto k PCs: Z = XVₖ. Best rank-k approximation (Eckart-Young-Mirsky).
Total variance = sum of eigenvalues. Explained variance ratio = λₖ/Σλᵢ.
First PC maximizes Rayleigh quotient vᵀSv subject to ||v|| = 1.

edgeCases.q: What if features have different scales?
edgeCases.a: PCA is not scale-invariant. Features with larger variances dominate. Always standardize (z-score) features before PCA if they're on different scales or units. Otherwise, height in cm vs weight in kg gives nonsense components.

edgeCases.q: What happens when n < p (more features than samples)?
edgeCases.a: Covariance matrix is singular (at most n-1 nonzero eigenvalues). Standard eigendecomposition fails, but SVD works fine. At most n-1 meaningful principal components exist; the rest have zero variance (numerically: very small).

edgeCases.q: How does PCA relate to SVD?
edgeCases.a: SVD of centered X gives PCA: X = UΣVᵀ, then XV = UΣ gives PC scores. Covariance S = (1/(n-1))VΣ²Vᵀ. SVD is numerically preferred: computing XᵀX squares condition number; SVD of X is stable.

sources.label: Jolliffe & Cadima — Principal component analysis: a review
Shlens — A Tutorial on Principal Component Analysis
